_comment: Agent-authored Profile spec — the DYNAMIC content layer. Built fresh by build-profile from signals.json + patterns.json. Inline values are a snapshot until the next rebuild.

boxes.text: 40,355 events across 357 sessions, 41 projects, Apr–Jun 2026 — driven through two agents. Opencode carries more sessions (202 vs 155) but Claude does the heavy lifting: 32,047 of the events. The model split is the tell — Claude is one-speed (opus-4-8 x12,803), while opencode routes freely to local Qwen, so the downshift habit exists on one agent and not the other.

items.label: events
items.note: all normalized events across both agents

row.title: Sessions by agent
row.note: Opencode runs more sessions; Claude runs longer ones — 32,047 events vs 8,308.

row.title: Events by agent
row.note: Claude is where the per-turn work lands despite fewer sessions.

boxes.title: Model usage (calls)
boxes.note: Opus dominates the Claude side; fable-5 (x1,201) and sonnet-4-6 (x80) barely register. The Qwen tiers are opencode's local routing — proof the downshift reflex works, just not on Claude.

boxes.title: Top projects by sessions
boxes.note: route-optimization-nvidia-cuopt is the center of gravity (85 sessions); the long tail of 41 projects is mostly one-offs.

items.name: Opus runs every Claude task, downshift gear untouched
items.frequency: claude-opus-4-8 x12,803 vs fable-5 x1,201 / sonnet-4-6 x80; /model switched only 9x and /effort 3x across 357 sessions.
items.payback: directional — can't see per-call difficulty from usage alone, but the routing-cadence gap is stark; try a week of deliberate downshifting
items.detail: Make downshifting a conscious default for mechanical Claude work instead of leaving it all on Opus.

boxes.text: 127 active hours, and nearly half of them (48.1%, 61.2h) are spent watching the agent run. 167 of 357 sessions overlap with another at some point (max concurrency 4), and 317 sit inside a chain — long pushes fragment into back-to-back relaunches rather than one continued thread. The gap histogram peaks at overlap and sub-5-minute resets: the relaunch tax is real.

items.label: spent waiting
items.note: active wall-time idle on the agent — the single biggest lever

items.label: babysitting
items.note: total wait hours watching turns finish

boxes.title: Wait hours by project
boxes.note: route-optimization-nvidia-cuopt alone is 25.1h of wait over 488 turns (mean 185s/turn). The slow-feedback projects (CUDA build / CI / heavy tests) carry the babysitting.

boxes.title: Gap between consecutive sessions
boxes.note: 89 overlaps + 93 sub-5-minute gaps dominate — long efforts chopped into fresh-context relaunches, each re-explaining setup. The 2026-06-28 chain was 18 jigsmith sessions in 77.5 min.

items.label: project switches
items.note: chain handoffs that switch project (vs 185 same)

items.name: Half your active wall-time is spent watching the agent run
items.frequency: 61.2h total wait over 127h active (48.1% of active wall-time); p90 turn = 421s, mean 155.8s across 1,414 turns; route-optimization alone = 25.1h wait / 488 turns.
items.payback: strong directional — 48% idle wall-time is the largest single lever in the whole rollup; scope the slowest 3 projects first
items.detail: Shrink the feedback loop on slow projects: background long-runners, swap full builds for scoped incremental checks.

items.name: Cross-project juggling pays a re-context tax on every switch
items.frequency: 46 cross-project overlap episodes; 72 chain handoffs that switch project (vs 185 same); 27,398s cross-project overlapped wall.
items.payback: directional — 72 switch-handoffs; per-project loader pays back if juggling continues
items.detail: Give each frequently-juggled repo a one-shot launch/context jig so a switch costs seconds, not a re-onboarding.

items.name: One effort chopped into a flurry of fresh-context relaunches
items.frequency: 317 of 357 sessions in a chain; gap buckets 0-5min x93 and overlap(<0) x89 dominate; 2026-06-28 = 18 sessions / 77.5 min on jigsmith.
items.payback: directional — 93 sub-5-min relaunches; one memory file removes the re-onboarding on each
items.detail: Stabilize a project memory / CLAUDE.md so a relaunch inherits setup instead of re-explaining it.

boxes.text: 1,529 turns, 10,542 tool calls — median 2 per turn but a long tail to 138. The grammar is maker-heavy: edit→edit (x1,319) chains changes with no check between, and verify-after-change (692) runs roughly once per four changes (2,961 edits+writes). Delegation is rare — only 44 turns fan out — so the longest turns (138/137/128 calls) grind independent work through one serial thread.

items.label: longest turn
items.note: tool calls in a single autonomous turn, 0 subagents

items.label: verify-after-change
items.note: ~1 check per 4 changes — the checker rarely fires

items.label: delegation turns
items.note: of 1,044 tool turns; 68 subagent calls total

boxes.title: Tool-calls per turn (leash)
boxes.note: 149 turns run 20+ tool calls on a long leash — the autonomous marathons where comprehension debt accrues. Most turns stay short, but the heavy tail is where the unverified shipping happens.

row.title: Tool kinds (totals)
row.note: Maker tools (edit 2,405 + write 556) outweigh bash-as-verify; read 2,546 confirms heavy exploration.

row.title: Top transitions
row.note: edit→edit (1,319) is change-on-change with no check between; edit→bash (547) is the verify that does happen.

items.name: The loop edits 4x more than it checks
items.frequency: verify-after-change 692 vs ~2,961 change ops (2,405 edits + 556 writes) ≈ 1 verify per 4 changes; across 1,044 tool-using turns.
items.payback: high — fires on ~2,961 change ops; catches breakage the loop currently ships unverified, against a one-time hook setup
items.detail: Add a PostToolUse verify hook on Edit/Write so the loop self-checks instead of you eyeballing every diff.

items.name: Marathon serial turns and hand-coordinated session swarms instead of a sub-agent harness
items.frequency: 149 turns in the 20+ tool-call bucket; top-10 longest avg ~100 tool calls with 0-1 sub-agents; delegation in only 44 of 1,044 turns; 71 same-project overlap episodes.
items.payback: directional — only the independent slices parallelize, but it covers two lenses' worth of frequency (149 long turns + 71 swarm episodes) and the engine is built-in
items.detail: A thin delegation skill that triggers built-in subagents/Workflows to fan independent work out of long serial turns and same-project swarms.

boxes.text: Context weight is light — about 8KB of memory across four files (your own repo's scaffold excluded). The friction is elsewhere: 5 MCP servers are configured but only 4 ever fire, so graphify pays a schema tax on every turn it's never used. And standing instructions get re-typed as long preambles — the scanner and build-profile invocations repeat 5 and 4 times, boilerplate that belongs inside the skill.

items.label: memory files
items.note: excludes Jigsmith's own CLAUDE.md (the tool measuring itself)

items.label: memory bytes
items.note: modest footprint; contract-management's CLAUDE.md is the bulk

items.label: MCP configured
items.note: servers wired into config across agents

items.label: MCP exercised
items.note: graphify never called — taxes context every turn

boxes.title: Memory files by size (bytes)
boxes.note: Per-project + global memory only; the Jigsmith repo's own 10.4KB CLAUDE.md is dropped — it's the tool's constitution, not your workflow weight.

boxes.title: Repeated preambles (repeats)
boxes.note: Standing instructions re-entered into the window each run. The scanner/build-profile preambles are fixed skill behavior that should live in the skill, not the prompt.

items.name: Long standing instructions re-typed to invoke Jigsmith skills
items.frequency: scanner-ANALYZE preamble x5, build-profile preamble x4 (9 combined repeats).
items.payback: directional — 9 combined repeats of avoidable boilerplate; trims the window each run, near-zero cost since the skills already exist
items.detail: Bake the standing 'don't re-run the miner / read these files' instructions into the skills so invocation shrinks to a slash call.

items.name: Re-typed 'generate a mock .xlsx' setup every time
items.frequency: the 'Use the desktop-commander mcp to generate a .xlsx file with generated mock data' preamble x4.
items.payback: directional — 4 repeats so far; worth it if mock-data generation keeps recurring
items.detail: Forge a 'mock-xlsx' skill that takes columns/rows and emits the file from a column spec.

boxes.text: The rig is lean and mostly live: 1 skill, 1 agent, 5 MCP servers installed, and nearly everything gets used. The two exceptions read as dead config — the graphify MCP (0 calls, but pays rent every turn) and the Architect subagent (0 dispatches). MCP traffic is dominated by selenium (239) and mssql (203); subagent work runs almost entirely through Explore (52).

items.label: MCP exercised
items.note: graphify is the one never-called server

boxes.title: MCP servers by usage
boxes.note: Four of five servers fire constantly; graphify (0) is absent — it's the non-code graph tool, distinct from gitnexus (44) which you do use here.

row.title: Skills invoked
row.note: build-profile leads — the dogfood loop. Most skills are one-off invocations.

row.title: Subagents dispatched
row.note: Explore carries the real subagent load; Architect (the installed config) never appears.

items.name: graphify MCP server loads every turn, never called
items.frequency: 0 invocations across the whole mined window; the other servers fire constantly (selenium x239, mssql x203, desktop-commander x55, gitnexus x44, mcp-atlassian x26).
items.payback: immediate — every turn of every session stops paying graphify's schema tax; near-zero cost to act, trivially reversible
items.detail: Disable or remove the graphify MCP server from the config that loads it, reclaiming context tokens on every turn.

items.name: Architect subagent configured but never dispatched
items.frequency: 0 dispatches in the window; Explore x52 and general-purpose x6 carry the real subagent load.
items.payback: low — leave-alone: craft-flagged design role, cheap to keep; do not forge over it, verify with the dev before retiring
items.detail: Leave Architect in place unless you confirm it isn't your go-to for the occasional system-design session.

boxes.text: 4,283 shell entries over 85 active days, only 26.5% during a session — most hand-work happens around the agent, not inside it. The vocabulary is git-first (960), then cd (847) and nvim (465), and the full commands show fixed rituals retyped verbatim: a three-step stage→commit→push, the same long cd paths, source .venv/bin/activate x103, and a long claude_local launch line x32. Plus three installed tools sitting unused.

items.label: during session
items.note: the other 73.5% is around/between agent runs

boxes.title: Top verbs
boxes.note: Three verbs (git/cd/nvim) account for the bulk of manual work — version control, navigation, and config editing, all candidates for aliases or jump tools.

row.title: git subcommands
row.note: add 145 / commit 276 / push 198 cluster as one repeated 3-step sequence, often with throwaway messages ('tweaks' x14).

row.title: Shell categories
row.note: Version control and pure navigation lead — both highly mechanical, both collapsible to shortcuts.

boxes.title: Most-typed full commands
boxes.note: Fixed strings retyped dozens of times each: the same long cd paths, redundant venv activation (uv already drives the env), and the bare git steps — the raw material for aliases and a frecency cd.

items.name: Hand-driven stage → commit → push ritual
items.frequency: git add . x138, git commit x276 (e.g. "tweaks" x14), git push x198 over 85 active days (~3 commits/day); git is your #1 verb at 960 total.
items.payback: 3 cmds × ~3/day × 85 days ≈ 760 saved invocations vs a ~10-min one-line function — pays back in days
items.detail: One `gacp "msg"` that runs add . → commit → push, defaulting the message when omitted.

items.name: Long claude_local launch typed by hand
items.frequency: the full long form x32, plus `claude-local` x55 and verbs claude-local 56 / claude_local 35 — a daily launch ritual.
items.payback: ~32+ retypes of a long fixed string vs a 1-line alias — trivially positive
items.detail: Bake the --url and --model into a single `qwen` alias (or fold into the existing claude-local wrapper).

items.name: Hand-editing the same config files
items.frequency: `nvim ~/.bashrc` x50, `nvim ~/.config/opencode/opencode.json` x44 (nvim is verb #3 at 465); `source ~/.bashrc` x25 follows the bashrc edits.
items.payback: ~94 full-path retypes + 25 reloads vs two one-line aliases — positive within weeks

items.name: Repeated cd into the same project dirs
items.frequency: navigation x853 (cd is verb #2 at 847); `cd ~/projects/contract-management/` x103, route-optimization x23, ai-coding-snippets x21, ai-code-review x15, QA x14.
items.payback: directional — 850+ navigations; try zoxide a week, near-zero build cost since it's a download
items.detail: Adopt zoxide so `z contract` jumps to the frecent dir instead of typing the full path.

items.name: Manual venv activation despite using uv
items.frequency: `source .venv/bin/activate` x103; source verb x129; uv x194 (uv run pytest, uv sync).
items.payback: directional — 103 activations gone; try `uv run` for a week or wire one .envrc
items.detail: Lean on `uv run ...` (no activate needed) or add direnv to auto-activate on cd into a project.

items.name: Installed tools sitting unused
items.frequency: installed_unused x3 of 7 installed: cli, desktop-commander, gcc.
items.payback: low-stakes hygiene; window-bounded, so confirm before removing
items.detail: Review desktop-commander for removal; leave gcc/cli (likely latent toolchain) unless confirmed dead.
